skeletal.description: The skeletal system provides structural support and protection for vital organs, enables movement through joints and muscles, and produces blood cells within bone marrow.

cardiovascular.description: The cardiovascular system is the body's transport network, pumping oxygenated blood to every cell and returning deoxygenated blood to the lungs.

respiratory.description: The respiratory system facilitates the essential exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the body and the environment.

digestive.description: The digestive system breaks down food into nutrients, absorbs them into the bloodstream, and eliminates waste along the GI tract.

nervous.description: The nervous system is the body's command center, processing sensory information and coordinating responses through electrical and chemical signals.

urinary.description: The urinary system filters waste products from the blood, regulates blood volume and pressure, and controls levels of electrolytes and metabolites.

sensory.description: The sensory system processes environmental stimuli through specialized receptors, transmitting sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch data to the brain.

skeleton.description: The complete framework of bones and cartilage that protects organs and allows movement.
skeleton.function: Provides structural support, protects internal organs, acts as levers for muscles, and stores minerals.
interestingFacts: Babies are born with around 270 bones, which fuse to 206 by adulthood.
Bone is dynamic, living tissue that completely remodels itself every 10 years.

skull.description: A bony structure that forms the head in vertebrates, supporting the structures of the face and providing a protective cavity for the brain.
skull.function: Protects the brain, houses sensory organs for vision, hearing, smell, and taste, and provides attachment points for facial muscles.
skull.location: Superior end of the vertebral column.
interestingFacts: The skull consists of 22 bones, divided into 8 cranial bones and 14 facial skeleton bones.
The mandible is the only bone in the skull that is fully movable.

spine.description: The central axis of the skeleton, consisting of a flexible column of vertebrae.
spine.function: Protects the spinal cord, supports the weight of the head and torso, and allows for flexible body movements.
spine.location: Dorsal midline of the body, extending from the base of the skull to the pelvis.
interestingFacts: The human spine contains 33 vertebrae at birth, which fuse to form 26 bones in adulthood.
Humans and giraffes have the exact same number of cervical vertebrae in their necks.

ribcage.description: An arrangement of bones in the thorax formed by the sternum and ribs.
ribcage.function: Protects the lungs, heart, and major blood vessels, and assists in respiration by expanding and contracting.
ribcage.location: Thoracic region of the body, surrounding the chest cavity.
interestingFacts: Most humans have 12 pairs of ribs, but some individuals are born with an extra cervical rib.
The first seven pairs of ribs are called 'true ribs' because they connect directly to the sternum.

heart.description: A hollow muscular organ that pumps blood through the circulatory system.
heart.function: Pumps oxygenated blood containing oxygen and nutrients to tissues and organs, and returns deoxygenated blood to the lungs.
heart.location: Thoracic cavity, left-central chest area between the lungs.
interestingFacts: The heart beats approximately 100,000 times a day, circulating about 7,500 liters of blood.
Heart cells cease dividing shortly after birth, meaning the heart has extremely limited self-repair capabilities.

lungs.description: A pair of spongy, air-filled organs located on either side of the chest.
lungs.function: Extracts oxygen from the atmosphere into the bloodstream and releases carbon dioxide from the bloodstream into the atmosphere.
lungs.location: Thoracic cavity, protected by the rib cage.
interestingFacts: The left lung is slightly smaller than the right lung to make room for the heart.
Lungs contain over 300 to 500 million tiny air sacs called alveoli.

liver.description: A large, lobed glandular organ in the abdomen, involved in many metabolic processes.
liver.function: Filters blood coming from the digestive tract, detoxifies chemicals, metabolizes drugs, and secretes bile that ends up in the intestines.
liver.location: Right upper quadrant of the abdomen, below the diaphragm.
interestingFacts: The liver is the only visceral organ that possesses the remarkable ability to regenerate itself completely from as little as 25% of its original tissue.
It performs more than 500 distinct functions vital to survival.

stomach.description: A muscular, elastic organ that digests food mechanically and chemically.
stomach.function: Stores swallowed food, mixes it with hydrochloric acid and digestive enzymes to break down proteins, and passes the semi-liquid chyme to the small intestine.
stomach.location: Upper left quadrant of the abdomen, superior to the small intestine.
interestingFacts: The stomach produces a new mucus lining every two weeks to prevent its highly acidic digestive juices from dissolving itself.
It can expand to hold up to 4 liters of food and liquid.

small_intestine.description: A long, narrow, folded tube connecting the stomach to the large intestine.
small_intestine.function: Performs 90% of chemical digestion and absorbs nutrients, minerals, and water from food into the bloodstream.
small_intestine.location: Central and lower abdominal cavity, framed by the large intestine.
interestingFacts: Despite being called 'small' due to its narrow width, it is about 6 meters long, which is three times the length of the large intestine.
The interior wall is lined with millions of microscopic projections called villi, increasing the surface area for absorption.

large_intestine.description: The final section of the gastrointestinal tract, wider than the small intestine.
large_intestine.function: Absorbs water and mineral salts from indigestible food residue, processes waste using gut bacteria, and stores feces prior to elimination.
large_intestine.location: Loops around the perimeter of the small intestine in the abdominal cavity.
interestingFacts: It houses trillions of beneficial bacteria, collectively called the gut microbiome, which weigh up to 2 kilograms.
The appendix, attached to the beginning of the large intestine, acts as a safe house for good bacteria.

brain.description: The complex control center of the nervous system, enclosed within the skull.
brain.function: Controls thoughts, memory, speech, motor function, emotion, and regulates vital automatic body systems and homeostasis.
brain.location: Cranial cavity of the skull, superior to the spinal cord.
interestingFacts: The brain consumes about 20% of the body's total energy budget, despite making up only 2% of the body's weight.
Information travels along cranial nerves at speeds of up to 430 kilometers per hour.

kidneys.description: A pair of bean-shaped organs that filter blood to produce urine.
kidneys.function: Filter waste products and excess fluid from blood, regulate systemic blood pressure, maintain electrolyte balance, and stimulate red blood cell production.
kidneys.location: Retroperitoneal space, on either side of the spine below the rib cage.
interestingFacts: Each kidney contains about one million filtering units called nephrons.
If you lose one kidney, the remaining one will increase in size by 50% to take over the workload.

eye.description: The primary sensory organ of the visual system.
eye.function: Detects light stimuli, converts it into electro-chemical signals, and transmits this data through the optic nerve to the brain for visual processing.
eye.location: Orbital cavities of the skull facial skeleton.
interestingFacts: The human eye can distinguish approximately 10 million distinct colors.
The cornea is the only tissue in the human body that contains no blood vessels; it receives oxygen directly from the air.

ear.description: The organ of hearing and equilibrium (balance) in vertebrates.
ear.function: Converts acoustic sound waves into nerve impulses, and senses changes in body position to coordinate physical balance and orientation.
ear.location: Lateral sides of the skull, housed within the temporal bones.
interestingFacts: The smallest bone in the human body, the stapes (stirrup), is located in the middle ear and is only 3 millimeters long.
Your ears never stop hearing, even when you are asleep; the brain simply filters out environmental sound signals.
